issues: color_temperature_mismatch: candidate is warm gold/tan dominant; peers are cool gray stone dominant
outline_color_mismatch: candidate uses ornate gold border outlines; peers use dark pixel outlines on stone tiles
rendering_style_divergence: candidate is clean decorative UI panel aesthetic; peers are gritty environmental dungeon tileset
palette_overlap_minimal: candidate palette (gold, beige, tan) shares almost nothing with peer palette (grays, blacks, teal, blood-red)
charm_level_mismatch: peers are grim dark dungeon aesthetic; candidate reads as approachable ornate RPG UI - different emotional register entirely
shadow_direction_inconsistent: peers imply top-left light source with pixel shading; candidate panel border has embossed multi-layer highlight/shadow inconsistent with peer lighting
highlight_intensity_divergence: candidate gold border has bright shiny highlights; peers are matte stone with dull shading
detail_density_inconsistent: candidate has dense ornate border but sparse interior; peers have uniform moderate tile-level detail throughout
reasoning: Across 11 dimensions the candidate inventory panel fails to match the peer dungeon tileset reference. The most critical breaks are color temperature (warm gold vs cool gray), outline treatment (ornate gold frame vs dark pixel outlines), and rendering philosophy (clean decorative UI vs gritty environmental pixel art). Palette overlap is near-zero - the only shared element is a small amount of gold visible in the peer sheet's statue sprites, but that is incidental. The candidate reads as a different game's UI layer dropped onto a dungeon tileset peer sheet. Charm level also diverges: peers project danger/grimness; candidate projects approachable inventory management. Shadow and highlight treatment further compounds the mismatch. A passing candidate would need to share the cool stone palette, muted highlights, and gritty pixel texture of the peer set. This candidate shares none of those properties at a primary level.
